content.text: Tiling and recomputation are the two implementation techniques that give FlashAttention its IO and memory efficiency. Tiling eliminates HBM round-trips during the forward pass by keeping intermediate results in SRAM. Recomputation eliminates storing the attention probability matrix for the backward pass by recomputing it on the fly. Together they reduce activation memory from O(N²) to O(N) while keeping the kernel compute-bound.
The core constraint is that one Q tile, one K tile, and one V tile must fit in SRAM simultaneously, along with the running state tensors (m, l, O accumulator):

On A100 with 20 MB SRAM and d = 64 (float16), the maximum block size is roughly B_r = B_c = 1280, far larger than the 64–128 typically used in practice. The actual limit is the number of registers available per thread block. FlashAttention uses B_r = B_c = 64 or 128 depending on d:

attrs.alt: FlashAttention tiling pattern: outer loop over Q tiles, inner loop over K/V tiles

content.text: The tiling algorithm has two nested loops. The outer loop iterates over Q tiles (rows of the attention matrix); the inner loop streams K and V tiles through SRAM. Only the Q tile stays resident in SRAM across the entire inner loop:

attrs.latex:
\text{Outer loop: } i = 0, 1, \ldots, T_r - 1 \quad (T_r = \lceil N/B_r \rceil)
\\
\text{Inner loop: } j = 0, 1, \ldots, T_c - 1 \quad (T_c = \lceil N/B_c \rceil)

content.text: Per inner iteration, the updates to the running state are:

attrs.latex:
\begin{aligned}
S_{ij} &= Q_i K_j^T / \sqrt{d} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{B_r \times B_c} \\
\tilde{m}_{ij} &= \text{rowmax}(S_{ij}) \in \mathbb{R}^{B_r} \\
m_i^{\text{new}} &= \max(m_i, \tilde{m}_{ij}) \\
\tilde{P}_{ij} &= \exp(S_{ij} - m_i^{\text{new}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{B_r \times B_c} \\
l_i^{\text{new}} &= e^{m_i - m_i^{\text{new}}} \cdot l_i + \text{rowsum}(\tilde{P}_{ij}) \\
O_i^{\text{new}} &= e^{m_i - m_i^{\text{new}}} \cdot O_i + \tilde{P}_{ij} V_j
\end{aligned}

content.text: After all T_c iterations, the output is normalised: O_i ← O_i / l_i, and both O_i and the log-sum-exp L_i = m_i + log(l_i) are written to HBM once.

import torch

def flash_attention_forward_tiled(
    Q: torch.Tensor,  # [N, d]
    K: torch.Tensor,  # [N, d]
    V: torch.Tensor,  # [N, d]
    Br: int = 64,
    Bc: int = 64,
    causal: bool = False,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    FlashAttention forward pass with explicit tiling.
    Returns (O, L) where L[i] = log-sum-exp for row i (needed for backward).
    """
    N, d = Q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5

    O = torch.zeros(N, d, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
    L = torch.zeros(N, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)   # log-sum-exp

    # Running state per row block (conceptually in SRAM registers)
    m = torch.full((N,), float("-inf"), dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
    l = torch.zeros(N, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)

    Tr = (N + Br - 1) // Br
    Tc = (N + Bc - 1) // Bc

    for i in range(Tr):
        rs, re = i * Br, min((i + 1) * Br, N)
        Qi = Q[rs:re]                          # [Br, d] — "stays in SRAM"
        Oi = torch.zeros(re - rs, d, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
        li = torch.zeros(re - rs, dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)
        mi = torch.full((re - rs,), float("-inf"), dtype=Q.dtype, device=Q.device)

        for j in range(Tc):
            cs, ce = j * Bc, min((j + 1) * Bc, N)

            # Skip tiles entirely above causal diagonal
            if causal and cs >= re:
                break

            Kj = K[cs:ce]                      # [Bc, d] — streamed through SRAM
            Vj = V[cs:ce]                      # [Bc, d]

            Sij = (Qi @ Kj.T) * scale          # [Br, Bc] — stays in SRAM (registers)

            if causal:
                rows = torch.arange(rs, re, device=Q.device).unsqueeze(1)
                cols = torch.arange(cs, ce, device=Q.device).unsqueeze(0)
                Sij = Sij.masked_fill(cols > rows, float("-inf"))

            m_tilde = Sij.max(dim=-1).values   # [Br]
            m_new = torch.maximum(mi, m_tilde)

            P_tilde = torch.exp(Sij - m_new.unsqueeze(-1))  # [Br, Bc]
            l_tilde = P_tilde.sum(dim=-1)      # [Br]

            rescale = torch.exp(mi - m_new)    # [Br]
            Oi = rescale.unsqueeze(-1) * Oi + P_tilde @ Vj
            li = rescale * li + l_tilde
            mi = m_new

        # Normalise and store output tile to HBM
        O[rs:re] = Oi / li.unsqueeze(-1)
        L[rs:re] = mi + torch.log(li)          # log-sum-exp, written once to HBM

    return O, L
Standard attention backward requires the full probability matrix P to compute gradients. FlashAttention's backward pass instead
P on the fly from the stored L vector (size N), running the same forward tiling pass a second time to regenerate each P tile when needed.
The backward pass computes gradients for Q, K, and V. The key intermediate is:
D_i is a scalar per output row, needed to compute the softmax gradient. The full backward gradient for Q, K, V within each tile is:

def flash_attention_backward_tiled(
    Q: torch.Tensor,   # [N, d]
    K: torch.Tensor,   # [N, d]
    V: torch.Tensor,   # [N, d]
    O: torch.Tensor,   # [N, d]  — forward output
    dO: torch.Tensor,  # [N, d]  — gradient of loss w.r.t. O
    L: torch.Tensor,   # [N]     — log-sum-exp from forward
    Br: int = 64,
    Bc: int = 64,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    FlashAttention backward: recomputes P tiles from L, avoids storing P.
    Returns (dQ, dK, dV).
    """
    N, d = Q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5

    dQ = torch.zeros_like(Q)
    dK = torch.zeros_like(K)
    dV = torch.zeros_like(V)

    # D[i] = rowsum(O[i] * dO[i]) — needed for softmax gradient
    D = (O * dO).sum(dim=-1)   # [N]

    Tr = (N + Br - 1) // Br
    Tc = (N + Bc - 1) // Bc

    for i in range(Tr):
        rs, re = i * Br, min((i + 1) * Br, N)
        Qi  = Q[rs:re]             # [Br, d]
        Oi  = O[rs:re]             # [Br, d]
        dOi = dO[rs:re]            # [Br, d]
        Li  = L[rs:re]             # [Br]
        Di  = D[rs:re]             # [Br]
        dQi = torch.zeros_like(Qi)

        for j in range(Tc):
            cs, ce = j * Bc, min((j + 1) * Bc, N)
            Kj = K[cs:ce]          # [Bc, d]
            Vj = V[cs:ce]          # [Bc, d]

            # Recompute Sij and Pij from stored L (not from stored S/P)
            Sij = (Qi @ Kj.T) * scale                    # [Br, Bc]
            Pij = torch.exp(Sij - Li.unsqueeze(-1))      # [Br, Bc]

            # Gradient of V
            dV[cs:ce] += Pij.T @ dOi                     # [Bc, d]

            # Gradient of S via softmax backward
            dPij = dOi @ Vj.T                             # [Br, Bc]
            dSij = Pij * (dPij - Di.unsqueeze(-1))        # [Br, Bc]

            # Gradient of Q and K
            dQi      += dSij @ Kj * scale                # [Br, d]
            dK[cs:ce] += dSij.T @ Qi * scale             # [Bc, d]

        dQ[rs:re] = dQi

    return dQ, dK, dV
Why Recomputation Is Cheaper Than Storing P

Storing the full N×N probability matrix P requires O(N²) HBM memory. Recomputing P on the backward pass costs one extra forward sweep (O(N²) FLOPs), but flops are cheap compared to HBM bandwidth. The trade-off is:
Read P again in backward: O(HN²)

For a 32-layer model with N = 4096, d = 64, H = 32, storing P costs 64 GB in memory. Recomputation costs one extra forward pass (roughly 33% slower backward, accepted in practice) but saves all 64 GB. The extra FLOPs are not the bottleneck since the backward kernel is also memory-bandwidth-bound for standard attention — and FlashAttention shifts it to compute-bound, so the extra FLOP cost is absorbed.
Each sequence in the batch runs independently. B × H thread blocks can run concurrently (one per head per sequence).
Each attention head has an independent Q, K, V set. H additional thread blocks run in parallel.

In FlashAttention-2, the outer loop over Q tiles is the primary parallelism axis. Each thread block handles one Q tile (one row block of the attention matrix) and processes all K, V tiles sequentially in its inner loop, reducing synchronisation.

Warps handle different columns of the B_r × B_c local attention score tile in parallel. FlashAttention-2 reorganised the work assignment to eliminate inter-warp communication for the online softmax update.

FlashAttention-1 parallelised over K, V tiles as the outer loop (one thread block per KV tile), requiring shared memory synchronisation for the Q tile. FlashAttention-2 swapped to Q tiles as the outer loop, making each thread block self-contained and eliminating the synchronisation bottleneck:
Using FlashAttention 2 with Custom Block Sizes
package exposes block sizes indirectly through the head dimension and CUDA kernel selection. For non-standard head dimensions, the package falls back to less optimised paths:

import torch
from flash_attn import flash_attn_func
from flash_attn.flash_attn_interface import _flash_attn_forward

# Standard usage: flash_attn_func handles block size selection internally
# Q, K, V: [batch, seqlen, nheads, headdim]
out = flash_attn_func(q, k, v, causal=True, softmax_scale=head_dim**-0.5)

# Verify FlashAttention is used via PyTorch SDPA
import torch.nn.functional as F
with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(
    enable_flash=True,
    enable_math=False,     # disable fallback to verify FA is running
    enable_mem_efficient=False,
):
    out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q_bthd, k_bthd, v_bthd, is_causal=True)

# Check supported head dimensions (FA2 CUDA kernels are specialised for d in {32,64,96,128,160,192,256})
SUPPORTED_HEAD_DIMS = {32, 64, 96, 128, 160, 192, 256}
head_dim = q.shape[-1]
if head_dim not in SUPPORTED_HEAD_DIMS:
    print(f"WARNING: head_dim={head_dim} not in {SUPPORTED_HEAD_DIMS} — FA2 will fall back to slow path")

Tiling introduces a subtle numerical challenge: each tile computes its own local softmax scale, and these must be merged exactly. The online softmax correction factor is computed in FP32 to avoid catastrophic cancellation:

import torch

def safe_online_softmax_merge(
    O_prev: torch.Tensor,   # (d,)  accumulated output in FP32
    m_prev: float,          # running max so far
    l_prev: float,          # running sum of exp(S - m)
    S_tile: torch.Tensor,   # (Bc,)  raw scores for this tile
    V_tile: torch.Tensor,   # (Bc, d)  value vectors for this tile
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, float, float]:
    """
    Merge one KV tile into the running (O, m, l) state.
    All internal accumulation in FP32 regardless of input dtype.
    """
    S_tile_f32 = S_tile.float()
    V_tile_f32 = V_tile.float()

    m_tile = S_tile_f32.max().item()
    m_new = max(m_prev, m_tile)

    # Correction factors — computed in FP32 to keep precision
    alpha = torch.exp(torch.tensor(m_prev - m_new, dtype=torch.float32))
    P_tilde = torch.exp(S_tile_f32 - m_new)   # (Bc,)

    l_new = alpha.item() * l_prev + P_tilde.sum().item()
    O_new = alpha * O_prev + P_tilde @ V_tile_f32   # (d,)

    return O_new, m_new, l_new


def finalize(O: torch.Tensor, l: float, target_dtype: torch.dtype) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Divide by sum and cast back to model dtype."""
    return (O / l).to(target_dtype)

BF16 is safer than FP16 for the score accumulation because BF16 has a wider exponent range (same as FP32), which prevents overflow in the softmax exponentials. On Ampere+ GPUs FlashAttention-2 accumulates the softmax numerator in FP32 even when input Q/K are BF16, incurring a small extra register cost but guaranteeing numerical stability.
max score of 12.0 → exp(12) ≈ 162755 — overflows FP16 (max ~65504). Always subtract the tile max before computing exp.
BF16 max representable ≈ 3.4 × 10¹³, well above any realistic score.
Q·Kᵀ computed in FP16/BF16, P·V accumulated in FP32, output cast back to input dtype. This is the default in flash_attn ≥ 2.0.
Verifying that your attention runs are actually using FlashAttention (and not PyTorch's slow-path) requires checking the kernel name in a profiler trace or observing memory usage:

import torch
from torch.profiler import profile, record_function, ProfilerActivity

def check_flash_attention_active(
    q: torch.Tensor,
    k: torch.Tensor,
    v: torch.Tensor,
) -> None:
    """
    Run one attention pass under the profiler and print which kernel was invoked.
    Expects q/k/v shape (B, H, N, d) on CUDA.
    """
    with profile(
        activities=[ProfilerActivity.CUDA],
        record_shapes=True,
        with_stack=False,
    ) as prof:
        with record_function("attention"):
            with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(
                enable_flash=True,
                enable_math=False,
                enable_mem_efficient=False,
            ):
                out = torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v)

    # Print CUDA kernels sorted by self CUDA time
    print(prof.key_averages().table(sort_by="self_cuda_time_total", row_limit=5))
    # FlashAttention kernels contain "flash" or "fmha" in the name
    events = [e for e in prof.key_averages() if "flash" in e.key.lower() or "fmha" in e.key.lower()]
    if events:
        print(f"FlashAttention kernel active: {events[0].key}")
    else:
        print("WARNING: FlashAttention kernel NOT found — check GPU, dtype, and head-dim constraints")
A quick way to confirm FlashAttention is active without a profiler: the peak CUDA memory during attention should scale as O(N) not O(N²). For N=4096, H=32, d=128 on FP16:

import torch

def memory_scaling_check(device: str = "cuda") -> None:
    """Compare peak memory for seq_len 2048 vs 4096. FA should scale ~2x; naive ~4x."""
    results = {}
    for N in [2048, 4096]:
        B, H, d = 1, 32, 128
        q = torch.randn(B, H, N, d, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)
        k = torch.randn(B, H, N, d, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)
        v = torch.randn(B, H, N, d, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)

        torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats(device)
        with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True, enable_math=False):
            _ = torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v)
        results[N] = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated(device) / 1024**2

    ratio = results[4096] / results[2048]
    print(f"Memory at N=2048: {results[2048]:.1f} MB")
    print(f"Memory at N=4096: {results[4096]:.1f} MB")
    print(f"Ratio: {ratio:.2f}x  ({'FA: O(N)' if ratio < 3 else 'NAIVE: O(N^2) — check config'})")
FlashAttention falls back to the PyTorch slow path silently under certain conditions. Understanding the fallback triggers helps diagnose unexpected OOM errors or slowdowns:
OOM for long sequences that used to fit
FA not active — using O(N²) naive attention
Check head_dim ≤ 256, contiguous tensors, supported dtype (FP16/BF16)
Fell back to math or mem-efficient kernel
Add enable_math=False, enable_mem_efficient=False to sdp_kernel context
Pass is_causal=True to scaled_dot_product_attention or flash_attn_func
Slow backward pass despite fast forward
Recomputation L vector not saved properly; memory layout issue
Ensure model uses flash_attn package (not SDPA wrapper) for full backward speed
CUDA error: head_dim must be a power of 2
Pad head_dim to next power of 2 (128, 256) or use FA3 which supports more dims

import torch

def diagnose_sdpa_backend(q, k, v, is_causal=False):
    """Report which SDPA backend will run and why."""
    # torch 2.1+: debug_sdpa_check returns a dict per backend
    with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(
        enable_flash=True, enable_math=True, enable_mem_efficient=True
    ):
        # Use the internal check (available in torch >= 2.1)
        try:
            from torch.backends.cuda import flash_sdp_enabled, mem_efficient_sdp_enabled, math_sdp_enabled
            q_c = q.contiguous()
            k_c = k.contiguous()
            v_c = v.contiguous()
            can_flash = (
                q_c.is_cuda
                and q_c.dtype in (torch.float16, torch.bfloat16)
                and q_c.shape[-1] <= 256
                and flash_sdp_enabled()
            )
            print(f"Flash SDP eligible: {can_flash}")
            print(f"  dtype ok: {q_c.dtype in (torch.float16, torch.bfloat16)}")
            print(f"  head_dim ok: {q_c.shape[-1]} <= 256: {q_c.shape[-1] <= 256}")
            print(f"  contiguous: {q_c.is_contiguous()}")
            print(f"  on CUDA: {q_c.is_cuda}")
        except ImportError:
            print("Torch < 2.1: manual inspection required")

The optimal block size Br × Bc is hardware- and sequence-length-dependent. Too small a block underutilises the tensor cores; too large a block spills registers and causes kernel stalls. A sweep reveals the sweet spot:

import time
import torch

def sweep_block_sizes(
    N: int = 4096,
    d: int = 128,
    device: str = "cuda",
) -> dict[int, float]:
    """
    Sweep over effective tile counts (proxy for block size) by varying sequence length
    at fixed N and observing throughput. In practice you'd pass block sizes to the
    Triton kernel directly; here we use N as a proxy.
    """
    B, H = 1, 16
    timings = {}
    for seq in [512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192]:
        q = torch.randn(B, H, seq, d, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)
        k = torch.randn(B, H, seq, d, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)
        v = torch.randn(B, H, seq, d, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)
        # Warmup
        for _ in range(3):
            torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
        torch.cuda.synchronize()
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        REPS = 20
        for _ in range(REPS):
            torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
        torch.cuda.synchronize()
        elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - t0) / REPS * 1000
        # FLOP: 4 * B * H * N^2 * d (attention matmuls)
        flop = 4 * B * H * seq * seq * d
        tflops = flop / (elapsed / 1000) / 1e12
        timings[seq] = tflops
        print(f"N={seq:5d}  latency={elapsed:.2f}ms  throughput={tflops:.2f} TFLOP/s")
    return timings
Triton-based FlashAttention implementations (including the open-source reference from Dao-AI-Lab) expose
as autotunable hyperparameters. The autotuner tries all combinations on first call and caches the best configuration per (head_dim, dtype, is_causal) in

Tiling divides the N×N attention computation into B_r × B_c blocks that fit in GPU SRAM, eliminating HBM round-trips for the intermediate score and probability matrices. Block sizes are chosen so that one Q tile, one K/V tile, and the running state (m, l, O accumulator) fit simultaneously in SRAM. The outer loop processes Q row tiles; the inner loop streams K, V column tiles through SRAM while the Q tile stays resident. Online softmax maintains a running maximum m and normalisation constant l that are rescaled on each inner iteration, making the tiling exact. Recomputation in the backward pass uses the stored log-sum-exp vector L (size O(N)) to regenerate each P tile on demand, trading one extra forward FLOP pass for eliminating the O(N²) P storage requirement. FlashAttention-2 improved parallelism by making Q tiles the outer parallel dimension, eliminating inter-warp synchronisation.
